justification: All 7 rules identified and 6/7 correctly classified. The 15-minute reservation timeout (BR-2) was marked invariant: false (reasoning: the duration is a tunable policy), but the rubric expects invariant: true (it is an inventory management rule — the mechanism of timed release is a state integrity constraint, not merely a policy knob). This single misclassification prevents a full pass since the rubric requires 'All 7 rules identified with correct classification.' All other pass criteria are met: refund window correctly invariant: false, cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly captured in BR-1, multiple ambiguities surfaced (discount scope per-order vs per-line-item, boundary conditions on time windows, partial checkout semantics, timer start point), and 12 acceptance criteria (well above the 4 minimum).

observations.text: Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction is the very first rule (BR-1) with strong justification about phantom stock-outs — no domain misconception here
Refund window (BR-7) correctly classified as invariant: false with clear policy reasoning (VIP extensions, holiday seasons, regulatory changes)
Discount code / promotion mutual exclusivity captured and further escalated as an ambiguity (per-order vs per-line-item scope)
12 acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format, covering happy paths, boundary conditions, and multi-item cart scenarios
9 edge cases identified including race conditions, mid-checkout promotion changes, and boundary timestamp behavior
Confidence level of 78 is well-calibrated with clearly articulated low-confidence areas and explicit assumptions

BR-2 (15-minute reservation timeout) classified as invariant: false. While the specific duration is tunable, the existence of a timed reservation release is a state integrity mechanism for inventory management — without it, reserved inventory leaks permanently. The rubric treats this as invariant: true.

The timer ambiguity is addressed in assumptions ('measured from checkout initiation, not from last user interaction') but not explicitly flagged as an escalation — the rubric specifically wanted 'Does the 15-minute timer start at checkout page load or first item addition?' surfaced as an ambiguity
Delivery definition ambiguity partially surfaced in edge case 8 ('Delivery date is disputed or not yet recorded') though not phrased as 'courier scan vs customer confirmation'
